title: Introduction to CompMod
text: Comparable company analysis, commonly called comps, one widely used valuation methods investment banking equity research. core idea straightforward. want estimate company worth, look market currently pricing similar public companies apply pricing ratios target. logic companies operating industry similar financial profiles trade similar multiples. practice, building comps model involves several repetitive error prone steps. analyst must import raw financial data Excel, verify recalculate valuation multiples first principles, summarize multiples across peer group establish valuation range. steps takes time introduces opportunities mistakes. CompMod built handle exactly workflow. provides three focused functions take raw Excel file peer company data produce clean, reproducible comps analysis just lines R code.

title: Introduction to CompMod
text: Load package:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("ADC-405-S26/CompMod") library(CompMod)

title: Introduction to CompMod
text: CompMod ships built dataset 15 fictional technology companies. company financial metrics needed run full comps analysis. dataset designed mirror structure real comparable company analysis model used investment banking equity research. dataset contains 15 companies 18 columns. columns cover share price, capital structure metrics like enterprise value net debt, income statement items like revenue, EBITDA, net income, earnings per share.
code: head(valuation_data[, c("Company.Name", "Ticker", "Share.Price",                          "Enterprise.Value", "Revenue..LTM.", "EBITDA")]) #>       Company.Name Ticker Share.Price Enterprise.Value Revenue..LTM. EBITDA #> 1    Xylo Dynamics    XYZ       48.25          28000.0         11800   2850 #> 2  Dogwood Systems    DOG       36.80          20435.0         11800   2180 #> 3         NovaGrid    NVG       29.60          21635.0         11800   1825 #> 4 BlueFox Software    BFX       22.40          12835.0         11800   1240 #> 5       TitanCloud    TCL       62.15          47380.0         11800   3360 #> 6        Apex Data    APX       18.75          10437.5         11800    910

what: Step 1 — Read your Excel file with read_valuation_excel()
title: Introduction to CompMod
text: read_valuation_excel() entry point analysis using data. function three things sequence. First reads Excel file R using readxl package. Second standardizes column names safe work R. example column called “Share Price” Excel file becomes Share.Price R. Third validates data checking required columns present key financial columns contain numeric values rather text blank cells. validation step important. Excel file missing required column contains non-numeric entries financial field, function stops immediately returns error message tells exactly needs fixed. means catch data problems silently affect analysis downstream. required columns : Company.Name, Ticker, Sector, Share.Price, Shares.Outstanding, Market.Capitalization, Net.Debt, Enterprise.Value, Revenue..LTM., EBITDA, Net.Income, EPS, Revenue.Growth.YoY, EBITDA.Margin. Excel file must follow structure function work correctly.
code: # install.packages("writexl") tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx") writexl::write_xlsx(valuation_data, tmp) df <- read_valuation_excel(tmp) head(df)

title: Introduction to CompMod
text: calc_multiples() takes validated data frame calculates five core trading multiples underlying financial data. Rather pulling pre-calculated values spreadsheet, function derives multiple independently. ensures numbers consistent reproducible regardless entered original Excel file. five multiples formulas : function also handles edge cases. denominator value zero returns warning know multiples may unreliable. value missing function propagates NA rather silently dropping .
code: comps <- calc_multiples(valuation_data) comps[, c("Company.Name", "EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA", "P.E", "EBITDA.Margin")] #> # A tibble: 15 × 5 #>    Company.Name     EV.Revenue EV.EBITDA   P.E EBITDA.Margin #>    <chr>                 <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl> #>  1 Xylo Dynamics         2.37       9.82  14.0        0.242  #>  2 Dogwood Systems       1.73       9.37  12.6        0.185  #>  3 NovaGrid              1.83      11.9   18.1        0.155  #>  4 BlueFox Software      1.09      10.4   12.6        0.105  #>  5 TitanCloud            4.02      14.1   20.3        0.285  #>  6 Apex Data             0.885     11.5   12.7        0.0771 #>  7 Quantum Byte          1.79      13.4   20.1        0.134  #>  8 Orion Tech            2.32      10.8   15.8        0.214  #>  9 Falcon AI             5.93      18.8   27.4        0.316  #> 10 EchoSoft              0.758     11.5   12.1        0.0657 #> 11 MetroLink             1.90      12.9   18.6        0.147  #> 12 Zenith Networks       2.59      13.3   19.5        0.195  #> 13 PixelWorks            1.21      14.1   18.1        0.0856 #> 14 RapidWare             1.66      14.1   19.5        0.118  #> 15 SkyVault              2.21      12.6   18.7        0.175

what: Step 3 — Summarise the peer group with peer_summary()
title: Introduction to CompMod
text: peer_summary() takes output calc_multiples() condenses summary table. multiple returns mean, median, minimum, maximum across peer group. summary table core output comps analysis. analyst uses median multiples derive implied valuation range target company. can also summarize specific subset multiples passing character vector multiples argument: default function removes NA values computing statistic. can change behavior setting na.rm = FALSE want NA values propagate summary.
code: peer_summary(comps) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   multiple        mean median     min    max #>   <chr>          <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 EV.Revenue     2.15   1.83   0.758   5.93  #> 2 EV.EBITDA     12.6   12.6    9.37   18.8   #> 3 P.E           17.4   18.1   12.1    27.4   #> 4 Price.Sales    1.82   1.51   0.430   5.61  #> 5 EBITDA.Margin  0.167  0.155  0.0657  0.316 peer_summary(comps, multiples = c("EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA")) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   multiple    mean median   min   max #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 EV.Revenue  2.15   1.83 0.758  5.93 #> 2 EV.EBITDA  12.6   12.6  9.37  18.8

title: Introduction to CompMod
text: full workflow raw Excel file peer group summary takes three lines code:
code: library(CompMod)  df    <- read_valuation_excel("valuation_data.xlsx") comps <- calc_multiples(df) peer_summary(comps)

title: Introduction to CompMod
text: peer group summary can apply median multiples target company’s financials estimate value. example, peer group median EV/EBITDA 12.0x target company EBITDA $500 million, implied enterprise value $6,000 million. subtract net debt get implied equity value, divide shares outstanding get implied share price. CompMod produces peer group statistics make calculation possible. final step applying statistics specific target left analyst, since judgment requires business context goes beyond data alone can provide.

what: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
text: CompMod R package comparable company analysis (comps). helps reading valuation data Excel, computing trading multiples, summarizing peer group statistics.

title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("ADC-405-S26/CompMod")

title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
code: library(CompMod)

title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
code: tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx") writexl::write_xlsx(valuation_data, tmp) df <- read_valuation_excel(tmp) head(df) #> # A tibble: 6 × 18 #>   Company.Name     Ticker Sector     Share.Price Shares.Outstanding #>   <chr>            <chr>  <chr>            <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Xylo Dynamics    XYZ    Technology        48.2                500 #> 2 Dogwood Systems  DOG    Technology        36.8                450 #> 3 NovaGrid         NVG    Technology        29.6                600 #> 4 BlueFox Software BFX    Technology        22.4                400 #> 5 TitanCloud       TCL    Technology        62.2                700 #> 6 Apex Data        APX    Technology        18.8                350 #> # ℹ 13 more variables: Market.Capitalization <dbl>, Net.Debt <dbl>, #> #   Enterprise.Value <dbl>, Revenue..LTM. <dbl>, EBITDA <dbl>, #> #   Net.Income <dbl>, EPS <dbl>, Revenue.Growth.YoY <dbl>, EBITDA.Margin <dbl>, #> #   P.E <dbl>, EV.Revenue <dbl>, EV.EBITDA <dbl>, Net.Income.Margin <dbl>

title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
code: comps <- calc_multiples(valuation_data) comps[, c("Company.Name", "EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA", "P.E")] #> # A tibble: 15 × 4 #>    Company.Name     EV.Revenue EV.EBITDA   P.E #>    <chr>                 <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 Xylo Dynamics         2.37       9.82  14.0 #>  2 Dogwood Systems       1.73       9.37  12.6 #>  3 NovaGrid              1.83      11.9   18.1 #>  4 BlueFox Software      1.09      10.4   12.6 #>  5 TitanCloud            4.02      14.1   20.3 #>  6 Apex Data             0.885     11.5   12.7 #>  7 Quantum Byte          1.79      13.4   20.1 #>  8 Orion Tech            2.32      10.8   15.8 #>  9 Falcon AI             5.93      18.8   27.4 #> 10 EchoSoft              0.758     11.5   12.1 #> 11 MetroLink             1.90      12.9   18.6 #> 12 Zenith Networks       2.59      13.3   19.5 #> 13 PixelWorks            1.21      14.1   18.1 #> 14 RapidWare             1.66      14.1   19.5 #> 15 SkyVault              2.21      12.6   18.7

title: Comparable Company Analysis Tools for Valuation
code: peer_summary(comps) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   multiple        mean median     min    max #>   <chr>          <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 EV.Revenue     2.15   1.83   0.758   5.93  #> 2 EV.EBITDA     12.6   12.6    9.37   18.8   #> 3 P.E           17.4   18.1   12.1    27.4   #> 4 Price.Sales    1.82   1.51   0.430   5.61  #> 5 EBITDA.Margin  0.167  0.155  0.0657  0.316

code: # install.packages("writexl") tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx") writexl::write_xlsx(valuation_data, tmp) df <- read_valuation_excel(tmp) head(df) #> # A tibble: 6 × 18 #>   Company.Name     Ticker Sector     Share.Price Shares.Outstanding #>   <chr>            <chr>  <chr>            <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Xylo Dynamics    XYZ    Technology        48.2                500 #> 2 Dogwood Systems  DOG    Technology        36.8                450 #> 3 NovaGrid         NVG    Technology        29.6                600 #> 4 BlueFox Software BFX    Technology        22.4                400 #> 5 TitanCloud       TCL    Technology        62.2                700 #> 6 Apex Data        APX    Technology        18.8                350 #> # ℹ 13 more variables: Market.Capitalization <dbl>, Net.Debt <dbl>, #> #   Enterprise.Value <dbl>, Revenue..LTM. <dbl>, EBITDA <dbl>, #> #   Net.Income <dbl>, EPS <dbl>, Revenue.Growth.YoY <dbl>, EBITDA.Margin <dbl>, #> #   P.E <dbl>, EV.Revenue <dbl>, EV.EBITDA <dbl>, Net.Income.Margin <dbl>

what: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
title: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
text: Takes data frame comparable companies (returned read_valuation_excel()) computes re-derives five core trading multiples used comparable company analysis: EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA, P/E, Price/Sales, EBITDA margin.

title: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
code: calc_multiples(data)

title: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
text: data data frame tibble containing minimum columns Company.Name, Ticker, Enterprise.Value, Revenue..LTM., EBITDA, Market.Capitalization, Net.Income, EPS, Share.Price, Shares.Outstanding.

title: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
text: tibble rows data plus five computed columns: EV.Revenue Enterprise Value divided LTM Revenue. EV.EBITDA Enterprise Value divided EBITDA. P.E Share Price divided EPS. Price.Sales Market Capitalization divided LTM Revenue. EBITDA.Margin EBITDA divided LTM Revenue.

title: Calculate key valuation multiples for a peer group — calc_multiples
code: comps <- calc_multiples(valuation_data) comps[, c("Company.Name", "EV.EBITDA", "EV.Revenue", "P.E")] #> # A tibble: 15 × 4 #>    Company.Name     EV.EBITDA EV.Revenue   P.E #>    <chr>                <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 Xylo Dynamics         9.82      2.37   14.0 #>  2 Dogwood Systems       9.37      1.73   12.6 #>  3 NovaGrid             11.9       1.83   18.1 #>  4 BlueFox Software     10.4       1.09   12.6 #>  5 TitanCloud           14.1       4.02   20.3 #>  6 Apex Data            11.5       0.885  12.7 #>  7 Quantum Byte         13.4       1.79   20.1 #>  8 Orion Tech           10.8       2.32   15.8 #>  9 Falcon AI            18.8       5.93   27.4 #> 10 EchoSoft             11.5       0.758  12.1 #> 11 MetroLink            12.9       1.90   18.6 #> 12 Zenith Networks      13.3       2.59   19.5 #> 13 PixelWorks           14.1       1.21   18.1 #> 14 RapidWare            14.1       1.66   19.5 #> 15 SkyVault             12.6       2.21   18.7

what: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
title: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
text: Computes descriptive statistics (mean, median, minimum, maximum) key trading multiples across peer group comparable companies. summary table core output comparable company analysis.

title: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
code: peer_summary(   data,   multiples = c("EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA", "P.E", "Price.Sales", "EBITDA.Margin"),   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
text: data data frame tibble contains multiple columns summarise. Typically output calc_multiples(). multiples character vector column names summarise. Defaults c("EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA", "P.E", "Price.Sales", "EBITDA.Margin"). na.rm Logical scalar. TRUE (default), NA values removed computing statistic.

title: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
text: tibble one row per multiple four statistic columns: multiple Name valuation multiple. mean Arithmetic mean across peer group. median Median across peer group. min Minimum observed value. max Maximum observed value.

title: Summarise peer group valuation multiples — peer_summary
code: comps <- calc_multiples(valuation_data) peer_summary(comps) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   multiple        mean median     min    max #>   <chr>          <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 EV.Revenue     2.15   1.83   0.758   5.93  #> 2 EV.EBITDA     12.6   12.6    9.37   18.8   #> 3 P.E           17.4   18.1   12.1    27.4   #> 4 Price.Sales    1.82   1.51   0.430   5.61  #> 5 EBITDA.Margin  0.167  0.155  0.0657  0.316  peer_summary(comps, multiples = c("EV.Revenue", "EV.EBITDA")) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   multiple    mean median   min   max #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 EV.Revenue  2.15   1.83 0.758  5.93 #> 2 EV.EBITDA  12.6   12.6  9.37  18.8

what: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
title: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
text: Reads standardized comparable company analysis Excel file returns clean tibble. function validates required columns present key numeric columns contain non-negative values, ensuring data ready downstream analysis.

title: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
code: read_valuation_excel(path, sheet = 1)

title: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
text: path length-one character string giving path .xlsx file. sheet length-one character string positive integer specifying sheet read. Defaults 1 (first sheet).

title: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
text: tibble one row per company. Column names standardized using make.names() safe use R expressions.

title: Read and validate a valuation Excel file — read_valuation_excel
code: tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx") writexl::write_xlsx(valuation_data, tmp) df <- read_valuation_excel(tmp) head(df) #> # A tibble: 6 × 18 #>   Company.Name     Ticker Sector     Share.Price Shares.Outstanding #>   <chr>            <chr>  <chr>            <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Xylo Dynamics    XYZ    Technology        48.2                500 #> 2 Dogwood Systems  DOG    Technology        36.8                450 #> 3 NovaGrid         NVG    Technology        29.6                600 #> 4 BlueFox Software BFX    Technology        22.4                400 #> 5 TitanCloud       TCL    Technology        62.2                700 #> 6 Apex Data        APX    Technology        18.8                350 #> # ℹ 13 more variables: Market.Capitalization <dbl>, Net.Debt <dbl>, #> #   Enterprise.Value <dbl>, Revenue..LTM. <dbl>, EBITDA <dbl>, #> #   Net.Income <dbl>, EPS <dbl>, Revenue.Growth.YoY <dbl>, EBITDA.Margin <dbl>, #> #   P.E <dbl>, EV.Revenue <dbl>, EV.EBITDA <dbl>, Net.Income.Margin <dbl>

title: Comparable company valuation dataset — valuation_data
text: dataset containing financial metrics valuation multiples 15 fictional technology companies, designed demonstrate functions CompMod package. data follows structure standard comparable company analysis (comps) model used investment banking equity research.

title: Comparable company valuation dataset — valuation_data
code: valuation_data

title: Comparable company valuation dataset — valuation_data
text: data frame 15 rows 18 columns: Company.Name Name fictional company (character). Ticker Stock ticker symbol (character). Sector Industry sector — companies Technology (character). Share.Price Current share price USD. Shares.Outstanding Total shares outstanding millions. Market.Capitalization Market cap millions USD. Net.Debt Total debt minus cash millions USD. Enterprise.Value Enterprise value millions USD. Revenue..LTM. Last twelve months revenue millions USD. EBITDA Earnings interest, taxes, depreciation amortisation millions USD. Net.Income Net income millions USD. EPS Earnings per share USD. Revenue.Growth.YoY Year--year revenue growth rate decimal. EBITDA.Margin EBITDA proportion revenue. P.E Price--earnings ratio. EV.Revenue Enterprise Value divided LTM Revenue. EV.EBITDA Enterprise Value divided EBITDA. Net.Income.Margin Net Income proportion revenue.
